message.content:
Forced Authentication involves adversaries compelling a user to automatically provide authentication information, which can be intercepted. This technique often exploits the Server Message Block (SMB) protocol in Windows networks, where systems automatically attempt to authenticate and send user credentials to remote systems. Web Distributed Authoring and Versioning (WebDAV) can also be exploited similarly. Adversaries may use spearphishing attachments with external resource links, specially crafted files (e.g., .SCF or .LNK files), or protocol handlers to force authentication and capture hashed credentials. These hashes can then be cracked offline to obtain plaintext credentials.

The potential impact includes unauthorized access to user accounts and sensitive information, leading to further network compromise. Common exploitation methods include Template Injection, spearphishing, and modifying file resources to trigger automatic authentication.

Mitigation strategies include disabling SMB and WebDAV where not needed, using strong, unique passwords to resist brute force attacks, monitoring and blocking outbound SMB traffic, and employing multi-factor authentication to reduce reliance on single-factor credentials. Regularly updating and patching systems can also help prevent exploitation of known vulnerabilities.
